showers.name: Quadrantids
showers.note: One of the strongest showers of the year, but with a very sharp peak lasting only about six hours — catch the right night or miss it entirely. Best after midnight in the northern sky.

showers.name: Lyrids
showers.note: The oldest recorded meteor shower — Chinese observations date back to 687 BC. Modest rates, but occasional outbursts of up to 100 per hour have been recorded.

showers.name: Eta Aquariids
showers.note: Debris from Halley’s Comet. Strongly favours the southern hemisphere and the tropics, where it’s one of the best showers of the year. Northern observers see far fewer, low on the horizon before dawn.

showers.name: Southern Delta Aquariids
showers.note: A steady mid-year shower best from the southern hemisphere. Lacks a sharp peak — decent rates across a couple of weeks, which makes planning easier.

showers.name: Perseids
showers.note: The most-watched shower of the year — warm August nights, high rates, and bright, fast meteors that often leave glowing trails. A northern-hemisphere summer classic.

showers.name: Orionids
showers.note: Halley’s second gift of the year. Visible from both hemispheres since Orion straddles the celestial equator. Fast meteors, best in the hours before dawn.

showers.name: Southern Taurids
showers.note: Low rates but famous for fireballs — unusually bright meteors that can outshine every star in the sky. The long broad peak overlaps its northern twin.

showers.name: Northern Taurids
showers.note: Twin stream of the Southern Taurids. When both branches are active in early November, fireball activity noticeably increases — worth a look even with low hourly rates.

showers.name: Leonids
showers.note: Modest in most years, but the source of history’s greatest meteor storms — in 1833 and 1966, rates briefly reached tens of thousands per hour. The next storm window is expected in the 2030s.

showers.name: Geminids
showers.note: The strongest reliable shower of the year, and unusual for coming from an asteroid rather than a comet. Slow, bright, often colorful meteors. Excellent from both hemispheres — worth braving the December cold.

showers.name: Ursids
showers.note: The year’s quiet closer, visible only from the northern hemisphere since the radiant sits near the pole star. Often overlooked in the post-Geminid lull.
